summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing its most severe residential electric bill increases in at least two decades, with a statewide average jump of 17.5% (roughly $28/month) between July 2024 and July 2025 — nearly double the prior year's record. Three of Indiana's five major investor-owned utilities (Duke Energy Indiana, NIPSCO, and CenterPoint Energy) sought rate increases exceeding $40/month in 2024-2025, while AES Indiana filed a fresh $192.9 million rate request in June 2025, even as NIPSCO customers now face the state's highest electric bills at $234/month average. The affordability crisis is compounded by threatened federal LIHEAP funding cuts, the elimination of LIHEAP program staff, and a growing wave of data-center-driven infrastructure costs that advocates warn could be shifted to residential ratepayers.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Low-income ratepayers and households receiving LIHEAP/EAP
messaging_recommendations.message: You are not alone — more than 49% of AES Indiana customers received a disconnection notice in the first half of 2025, and Indiana law gives you protection from winter shutoffs if you apply for the Energy Assistance Program. Knowing your rights and enrolling in available assistance programs can keep your lights on while advocates fight for lasting rate relief.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Silverman and Smith emphasize meeting audiences where they are with actionable, self-relevant information; pairing an alarming but validating statistic with a concrete protective step reduces feelings of helplessness and drives enrollment in safety-net programs.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Indiana state legislators and the Governor's office
messaging_recommendations.message: Indiana families are paying the highest year-over-year electric bill increases in 20 years — a problem driven by regulatory rules that reward utility investment regardless of customer impact. Performance-based ratemaking, stronger low-income discount programs, and a summer disconnection moratorium are proven, bipartisan solutions ready for the 2026 session.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Framing the issue in concrete dollar-harm terms (17.5% / $28/month average increase) and anchoring it to already-signed legislation (HEA 1002) demonstrates political feasibility and gives legislators a credible policy hook, consistent with Silverman and Smith's principle of aligning messages with decision-makers' existing commitments.

messaging_recommendations.message: Indiana's monopoly utilities are filing billion-dollar rate increases to build infrastructure for mega-corporations like Google and Amazon data centers, and those costs are on track to land on your monthly bill — while federal energy assistance for low-income Hoosiers is under threat of elimination.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Connecting a relatable, pocketbook concern (rising electric bills) to a larger injustice narrative (subsidizing wealthy tech companies) follows Silverman and Smith's strategic framing principle of linking individual experiences to systemic accountability, broadening the coalition of engaged citizens.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Community action agencies, faith communities, and social-service providers
messaging_recommendations.message: The energy affordability crisis is a health and housing crisis: families who can't pay utility bills face eviction, dangerous indoor temperatures, and impossible choices between food and electricity. Your clients need crisis fund enrollment, EAP applications, and your voice at IURC public hearings.
messaging_recommendations.rationale: Silverman and Smith stress the power of trusted messengers and multi-sector coalitions; mobilizing social-service providers expands advocacy reach into hard-to-reach populations and adds authentic human-impact testimony to regulatory proceedings.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana filed for a $491.5 million annual base rate increase — Indiana's largest-ever utility rate request at the time — to fund infrastructure investments and a multi-year capital plan. The IURC approved a reduced increase of approximately $295.7 million, phased in over two steps (approximately 8% in February 2025 and approximately 3% in early 2026), translating to an average 11% bill increase for residential customers.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately +$19.16/month average for residential customers (~11%); Step 1 effective February 27, 2025; Step 2 in early 2026

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed a $192.9 million base rate increase request in June 2025 — less than 14 months after its prior rate case was approved — seeking a phased increase totaling approximately 13.5% ($21/month) for a residential customer using 1,000 kWh by January 2027. The OUCC recommended denial and a rate reduction, while AES, the City of Indianapolis, Walmart, and industrial customers filed a partial settlement agreement in October 2025 reducing the requested increase to approximately $10/month; the OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition oppose the settlement.
rate_cases.rate_change: Requested: +~$21/month (13.5%) by 2027 in two phases; partial settlement would result in ~$10/month increase; OUCC recommends rate reduction

rate_cases.utility: NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) — Electric
rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed a petition on September 12, 2024, seeking a $368.7 million annual electric base rate increase — representing approximately 22% ($32/month) for an average residential customer. A settlement agreement was filed February 7, 2025, reducing the requested increase to approximately $257 million; the IURC approved the settlement on June 26, 2025, resulting in a phased increase of approximately $23/month (16.75%) beginning in July 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: +$23/month average residential (approximately 16.75%); phased in over multiple steps beginning July 2025

rate_cases.utility: NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) — Gas
rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed a gas base rate case requesting an increase of approximately $162 million annually. After the OUCC filed testimony supporting a settlement agreement, the IURC approved an increase of $120.9 million — $41 million less than NIPSCO's initial request — effective in two phases beginning August 2024 and Q1 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: +$5/month average for a residential customer using 72 therms (approximately 7.1%); phased in August 2024 and Q1 2025

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy filed for a $118.8 million annual base rate increase in December 2023. A settlement agreement was filed in 2024 reducing the increase to $80 million annually; the OUCC was not a party and filed opposition testimony. The IURC approved the settlement on February 3, 2025, resulting in a phased increase across three steps (approximately 6.5% in late 2024, 3.6% in early 2025, and 7.3% in early 2026), for a total of approximately 17.4%.
rate_cases.rate_change: +$80 million annually; approximately 17.4% total increase phased in over three steps through early 2026; average residential customer saw bills increase to approximately $189/month from $154/month

rate_cases.description: Indiana Michigan Power's 2023 rate case resulted in a settlement approved by the IURC in May 2024, allowing an annual increase of approximately $61.8 million phased in over two steps; the first effective May 2024 and the second adding approximately $4.27 beginning January 2025. I&M also received an IURC order in February 2025 in Cause No. 45159 related to a large-load tariff settlement covering interconnection rules for data centers.
rate_cases.rate_change: Phased increase totaling approximately $61.8 million annually; second phase of approximately $4.27/month effective January 2025; residential bill increase among the lowest of Indiana's five IOUs

articles.headline: Hoosiers' electric bills spike 17.5% in one year
articles.summary: A Citizens Action Coalition analysis of Indiana's five investor-owned electric utilities found that residential bills rose an average of 17.5% — roughly $28/month — between July 2024 and July 2025, marking the steepest year-over-year jump in at least two decades. NIPSCO customers were hit hardest, seeing a $50/month (26.7%) increase, compounding a 17.8% hike in 2024. CenterPoint followed with a $44/month average increase.

articles.headline: Consumer advocates: Spike in electric bills shows Indiana energy plan isn't working
articles.summary: Citizens Action Coalition Program Director Ben Inskeep said 2025 marked the highest year-over-year jump in Indiana electric bills in two decades, arguing that a 2023 state law requiring the IURC to weigh five factors — including affordability — has failed to curb rate increases. Inskeep highlighted that NIPSCO customers in northwest Indiana are absorbing nearly $50/month more than last year and called for stronger legislative action. The report was widely republished across Indiana public radio stations.

articles.headline: 'We can't take it anymore' | Gov. Braun appoints official to fight utility rate hikes in Indiana
articles.summary: Indiana Governor Mike Braun appointed Abby Gray as the new Utility Consumer Counselor and directed her to evaluate utility profits and find cost-saving measures to ease the burden on ratepayers. Braun simultaneously announced plans to reshape the IURC by filling vacancies with commissioners aligned with his affordability agenda. Two sitting IURC commissioners announced plans to resign early, giving the governor additional appointment opportunities.

articles.headline: High utility prices nearing crisis, and there are multiple factors behind the rise
articles.summary: Indiana Capital Chronicle reporter Niki Kelly reviewed the structural drivers of Indiana's utility cost surge, including tracker mechanisms that allow utilities to recover specific infrastructure costs outside of formal rate cases, growing data center electricity demand, and a regulatory rate-game in which utilities propose inflated hikes expecting to be negotiated down. The piece noted that so-called trackers now make up as much as 13.7% of a typical AES Indiana residential bill. The article called rising utility costs a more pressing crisis than the ongoing property tax debate.

articles.headline: AES rate hike case heats up as opposition builds
articles.summary: Opposition to AES Indiana's $192.9 million rate hike request intensified following the OUCC's recommendation that the IURC deny the increase entirely and instead reduce current rates by approximately $21 million. The Indianapolis City-County Council joined bipartisan opposition, citing both the rate hike and ongoing billing system problems tied to a 2023 software upgrade. Consumer advocates noted that nearly half of AES customers in Indianapolis received a disconnection notice in the first six months of 2025.

articles.headline: State agency recommends denial of AES Indiana's rate increase
articles.summary: The Indiana OUCC filed testimony recommending the IURC deny AES Indiana's $192.9 million base rate increase request, arguing that AES had not demonstrated sufficient evidence for a hike filed less than 14 months after its previous rate case was settled. The OUCC's filing included more than 6,800 written comments from AES customers and other stakeholders, along with accounts from 89 customers who spoke at four public hearings. The OUCC recommended the IURC instead reduce AES's current rates by roughly $21 million.

articles.headline: Indiana governor seeks lower utility rates: 'We can't take it anymore.'
articles.summary: Gov. Mike Braun publicly pressed Indiana's five major investor-owned utilities to lower rates, issuing a statement on September 3, 2025, and appointing a new Utility Consumer Counselor to scrutinize utility profits. Braun also initiated the process to replace multiple IURC commissioners with appointees aligned with his affordability priorities. Industry analysts from Capstone expressed doubt the governor's moves would produce meaningful rate cuts, particularly given NiSource's plans to build data-center power plants through a non-utility generating subsidiary.

articles.headline: Indiana electricity costs rise amid data center boom
articles.summary: A new Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis (IEEFA) analysis highlighted a dramatic spike in capacity market prices set at auction by PJM, the regional electric grid operator covering Indiana, largely driven by surging demand from data centers. The report underscored how Indiana's utility rate environment is being shaped not just by utility capital investment but by broader wholesale electricity market dynamics. Consumer advocates warned the trend will further stress household budgets as other costs of living continue to rise.

articles.headline: Ratepayer advocates recommend energy bill 'relief plan'
articles.summary: Indiana Conservation Voters and the Citizens Action Coalition jointly released a policy plan calling for income-based utility bill discounts (capped at a 3-4% income burden threshold), a summertime disconnection ban for low-income customers, performance-based regulation reforms, and restrictions on cost-shifting from large data-center customers to residential ratepayers. A Bellwether Research poll commissioned for the release found that approximately 87% of Hoosiers were at least 'somewhat' concerned about rising energy costs, with 59% 'very' concerned. The plan was timed ahead of the 2026 legislative session.

articles.headline: AES customers file opposition to rate hike settlement
articles.summary: The OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition filed formal opposition testimony against the AES Indiana partial settlement agreement, joined by more than 180 pages of consumer comments stating they cannot afford additional rate increases. The settlement — reached between AES, the City of Indianapolis, Walmart, Rolls-Royce, and major industrial customers — would result in a smaller residential rate increase than originally requested but still drew opposition from the state's ratepayer advocate and consumer groups who argued it was an unfair backroom deal that left residential customers exposed.

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: Hoosiers have been burdened with utility rate increase after increase. We can't take it anymore. I am directing the Utility Consumer Counselor, who is Indiana's ratepayer advocate, to evaluate utilities' profits along with other cost saving measures to ease the burden on ratepayers. I would also like to see the utilities' investors bear more of the cost of doing business.
stakeholders.source: Governor's press release / WTHR News report, September 3, 2025

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: AES Indiana has not demonstrated sufficient evidence for an increase in this rate case, which it filed less than 14 months after receiving Commission approval in its last rate case. The settlement agreement filed by AES and other parties shows the utility's initial request was inflated and unnecessary. The OUCC stands by its initial recommendations for a reduction from current rates and will oppose the utility's settlement agreement.
stakeholders.source: OUCC news release / Indiana Capital Chronicle, September 10, 2025 and October 16, 2025

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: Hoosiers have had enough with AES Indiana's poor service, soaring bills, and backroom deals. We will vigorously oppose this unfair and unaffordable deal and do everything we can to protect the residents of Indianapolis from yet another large utility bill increase. It's time for the IURC to send a message and restore affordability and accountability at our out-of-control monopoly utilities.
stakeholders.source: Citizens Action Coalition press release, October 2025

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: Affordability is at a crisis point when it comes to our electric utility bills. For example, NIPSCO customers in northwest Indiana are seeing a nearly $50 a month increase in just one year. Things keep getting worse for Hoosier consumers and our state leaders continue to allow it to happen. Based on this trend, the rhetoric regarding affordability is not matching the reality.
stakeholders.source: WFYI / Indiana Public Radio, July 24, 2025; WTHR News, September 2025

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: Indiana, like every state, is facing a real challenge when it comes to energy affordability. Costs are increasing across the board, and rising utility bills are placing a real burden on Hoosiers. In my short time as chairman of the IURC, my fellow commissioners and I have heard from thousands of utility customers from all over Indiana that they are struggling to pay their energy bills.
stakeholders.source: IURC press release on energy affordability investigative inquiry, February–March 2026; Journal Gazette interview

stakeholders.name: Megan Robertson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: Hoosier families are stretched thin, and they need relief now from monopoly utilities who've pushed policies for years that led to this crisis. This plan gives state leaders a clear, practical path to lower bills that will protect Hoosiers most in need and build a more affordable, reliable energy future for every community in Indiana.

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: For too long, Hoosiers have been footing the bill for decisions they didn't make and policies they didn't ask for. Indiana's regulatory system should be built to protect consumers, but that goal can only be achieved if lawmakers give regulators the tools and authority it needs. The solutions have been ready for years. The need is now incredibly urgent.

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: Nearly half — 49% — of AES customers in Indianapolis have been sent a disconnection notice through the first six months of 2025. They are granted the privilege of being a state-franchised monopoly with no competition, with profit embedded in monthly utility bills earning handsome returns. Meanwhile, the average everyday working class resident here in Indianapolis can't pay their bill.

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: AES Indiana filed a regulatory rate review with the Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission (IURC) on June 3, 2025 to continue making necessary and prudent investments to best serve our customers with cost-effective and reliable electric service. If approved by the IURC, a residential customer using 1,000 kWh per month will see an increase of about 13.5 percent — or up to $21 — in two phases. The regulatory rate review process will allow the IURC to carefully review the request.
stakeholders.source: AES Indiana press release / WTHR News, September 2025

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: I am deeply humbled and appreciative of Governor Braun's confidence in me and look forward to advocating for Indiana utility consumers.
stakeholders.source: Indiana Governor's office press release, September 3, 2025
